chunk_summary: Guidance on classifying and managing acute malnutrition in children 6–59 months, emphasizing referral pathways for severe cases and structured follow-up for moderate cases during complementary feeding.
learning_objectives: Understand WHO criteria for classifying severe and moderate acute malnutrition (SAM and MAM).
Identify complications requiring urgent referral for facility-based management of SAM.
Develop feeding plans for uncomplicated wasting and moderate malnutrition.
Recognize the importance of follow-up and catch-up feeding to prevent relapse.
key_facts: Severe acute malnutrition (SAM) is defined by WHZ < -3, MUAC <115 mm, or bilateral pitting edema.
Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) is defined by WHZ between -3 and -2 and/or MUAC 115–125 mm without edema.
Complications like lethargy, dehydration, or severe anemia require urgent referral to inpatient care.
Uncomplicated wasting requires outpatient care with therapeutic foods, energy-dense feeding, and structured follow-up.
Families often rely on diluted milk or watery gruels; nutrient-dense, thick feeds are essential.
Reassess moderate wasting within 7–14 days; escalate care if no weight gain or new symptoms appear.
reference_quote: WHO defines severe acute malnutrition (SAM) by any of: very low weight-for-length/height (WHZ < -3), bilateral pitting edema, or MUAC <115 mm.
